content.text: Launching an AI feature is not a finish line — it is the beginning of a measurement period. The hours and days immediately following a launch surface failure modes that no amount of pre-launch testing reliably predicts: unexpected usage patterns, latency behavior under real traffic shape, and user satisfaction with outputs that scored well on your eval suite but disappoint in context.
Post-launch reviews

are structured, time-boxed assessments at T+1 day, T+1 week, and T+30 days that answer the question: "Is this launch safe to expand, hold, or roll back?" — with enough rigor to make the answer defensible.

attrs.alt: Post-launch review cadence: T+1 Day (stability check) → T+1 Week (full metrics review) → T+30 Day (business impact review) → Feature Expansion decision, with action item tracker at bottom

content.text: What It Is
A post-launch review template is a set of structured meeting agendas and document templates that guide a team through three reviews at increasing scope: a
T+1 day stability check
(is the system stable?), a
T+1 week full review
(are metrics within projections?), and a
T+30 day business impact review
(did this move the metrics we care about?). Each review produces a structured output document and a prioritized action item list with owners and due dates.
Why Post-Launch Reviews Matter for AI Systems

AI systems surface failure modes under real traffic that synthetic testing misses. Real users ask questions with unusual phrasing, combine features in unexpected ways, and use edge cases as first-class interactions.

Latency behavior changes at scale. A model call that takes 800ms in single-user testing may take 1,800ms under real load due to provider-side queuing. Without a T+1 day latency review, this goes unnoticed.

Cost projections are frequently wrong. Token usage distributions are hard to predict from pre-launch testing, especially when users discover new interaction patterns. A T+1 week cost-per-request check prevents budget overruns from silently compounding.
Technical debt accumulates during launch. Shortcuts taken under time pressure — hardcoded limits, missing monitoring, deferred error handling — must be inventoried before they cause incidents.
The Three-Review Cadence
T+1 Day: Stability Check
Owner: on-call SRE lead. Duration: 30 minutes. Format: async async document review with optional brief sync.
Error rate vs baseline
: is the HTTP error rate, tool call failure rate, or model API error rate elevated above the pre-launch baseline? If error rate > 1%, this is a go/no-go signal.
P50 and P95 latency
: compare to the pre-launch benchmark. A P95 latency more than 50% above benchmark warrants investigation before expansion.
Incident count
: how many SEV-1, SEV-2, or SEV-3 incidents occurred in the first 24 hours? Any SEV-1 or SEV-2 incident is an automatic hold.
Rollback triggered
: binary Y/N. If a rollback was triggered, the T+1 day review is replaced by an incident post-mortem.
Cost vs projection
: is the cost per request within 20% of the pre-launch projection? More than 50% over projection is a hold signal.
T+1 Week: Full Metrics Review
Owner: PM and SRE tech lead. Duration: 60 minutes. Attendees: PM, engineering lead, SRE, support lead.
Latency P50 / P95 / P99
: all three percentiles over the full week. P99 is particularly important for AI systems — tail latency indicates the worst user experience.
User satisfaction score
: the aggregate thumbs-up rate, star rating average, or NPS from users who interacted with the new feature. Compare to the previous week's baseline.
Cost per request (actual vs projected)
: token usage breakdown by request type. Identify which usage patterns are more expensive than projected and whether they reflect valid user behavior or a misuse pattern.
Token usage distribution
: histogram of prompt + completion tokens per request. Unexpected heavy-tail usage (very long requests) signals users are using the system differently than anticipated.
Tool call success rate
: for agentic features, the percentage of tool calls that succeed on first attempt vs require retry or fail permanently.
Incident summary
: log of all incidents during the week with MTTD, MTTR, and category tag.
User feedback summary
: top themes from negative feedback, corrections received, and notable support escalations.
T+30 Day: Business Impact Review
Owner: PM and engineering manager. Duration: 90 minutes. Attendees: PM, engineering manager, design lead, support lead, finance if cost-sensitive.
Retention and engagement delta
: did the feature improve D7/D30 retention or session frequency for users who activated it, compared to a control cohort?
OKR contribution measured
: what did this feature contribute to the quarter's key results? Quantify: deflection rate +X%, latency p95 -Yms, NPS +Z points.
NPS / CSAT trend
: satisfaction trend over the 30-day period. Did satisfaction improve, stabilize, or decline as novelty effects wore off?
Monthly cost vs budget
: total LLM API cost, infrastructure cost, and cost per active user for the feature.
Technical debt inventory
: explicit listing of shortcuts taken during launch that must be addressed before feature expansion. Each item gets a severity (blocks expansion / should fix / nice to fix) and a size estimate.
Feature expansion go/no-go
: explicit go or no-go decision for the next expansion step (next user cohort, next geography, next feature scope), with the criteria that drove the decision documented.
Go / No-Go Criteria for Feature Expansion
Define expansion criteria before launch so the decision is data-driven, not negotiated in the review meeting:
Error rate < 1% (hard block if above)
No unresolved SEV-1 or SEV-2 incidents (hard block)
P95 latency within 30% of pre-launch benchmark
User satisfaction score ≥ pre-launch baseline
Cost per request within 30% of projection (or explicitly accepted by finance)
No expansion-blocking technical debt items outstanding
Post-Launch Review Document Template

# Post-Launch Review: [Feature Name]
**Review type**: T+1 Day / T+1 Week / T+30 Day  
**Review date**: YYYY-MM-DD  
**Author**: [name]  
**Attendees**: [list]

---

## Metrics Summary
| Metric | Pre-launch baseline | Launch target | Actual | Status |
|--------|-------------------|--------------|--------|--------|
| Error rate | X% | < 1% | Y% | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |
| Latency P50 | Xms | ≤ Xms | Yms | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |
| Latency P95 | Xms | ≤ Xms | Yms | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |
| Latency P99 | Xms | ≤ Xms | Yms | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |
| User satisfaction | X.X/5 | ≥ X.X/5 | Y.Y/5 | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |
| Cost per request | $X.XXX | ≤ $X.XXX | $Y.YYY | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |
| Tool call success | X% | ≥ X% | Y% | ✅ / ⚠️ / 🚫 |

## Incident Summary
| Date | Severity | Category | MTTD | MTTR | Status |
|------|----------|----------|------|------|--------|

## User Feedback Summary
**Top negative feedback themes:**
1. [theme] — N instances
2. [theme] — N instances

**Corrections received**: N  
**Support escalations related to feature**: N

## Technical Debt Identified During Launch
| Item | Severity | Effort | Blocks expansion? |
|------|----------|--------|-------------------|

## Action Items
| ID | Item | Owner | Due date | Priority |
|----|------|-------|----------|----------|

## Go / No-Go Decision
**Decision**: GO / NO-GO / CONDITIONAL  
**Condition (if conditional)**: ...  
**Next expansion step**: ...  
**Decision rationale**: ...
Key Metrics Reference for AI Systems
Latency P50/P95/P99
: P50 is median user experience; P95 is the "long tail" affecting 5% of users; P99 catches the worst 1%. For AI systems, P99 is often 5–10× P50 due to long-context requests.
Error rate

: percentage of requests that result in an error response (5xx, model refusal, tool call failure). Track by error type — a 2% error rate composed entirely of rate limit retries is less concerning than one composed of model hallucinations.
User satisfaction score
: aggregate of thumbs-up/down ratings, star ratings, or NPS for the feature. Compare weekly trend rather than absolute number to detect drift.
Cost per request
: total LLM API cost divided by request count. Track by user tier and feature flag to identify which cohorts or configurations are most expensive.
Deflection rate (for support AI)
: percentage of queries the AI resolves without human escalation.
Tool call success rate (for agentic features)
: percentage of tool calls that succeed without retry. A low first-call success rate indicates the model is generating malformed parameters.
Technical Debt Inventory During Launch
Every launch accumulates debt. Tracking it explicitly prevents it from becoming invisible. Categorize each debt item:
Blocks expansion
: the feature cannot be expanded to more users until this is addressed. Example: retry logic hardcoded to 3 attempts with no backoff — at 10× traffic this will cascade.
Should fix
: will cause problems eventually but not immediately. Example: missing structured logging on tool call failures makes incident diagnosis slow.
Nice to fix
: quality improvement with no immediate risk. Example: the prompt template has a manual repetition that could be refactored.
Tooling Integration
Common Mistakes
Skipping the T+1 day review
: teams under delivery pressure skip this review on the grounds that "the launch went fine." This is when undetected problems compound — the 24-hour window is when rollback is cheapest.
Using only P50 latency
: reporting only P50 (median) latency masks a bad tail experience. For AI systems, P99 can be 5–10× P50. Always report all three percentiles.
No pre-launch baseline documented
: if you don't capture baseline metrics before launch, you have nothing to compare against during the review. Capture baselines 1–2 weeks before launch day.
Technical debt not inventoried
: treating launch shortcuts as "temporary" without documenting them guarantees they become permanent. Explicitly list every shortcut in the T+1 week review.
Go/no-go criteria set during the review meeting
: criteria defined after seeing the data are not criteria — they are rationalizations. Define expansion criteria before launch.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Structured T+1/7/30 reviews (this pattern)
Systematic; forces metric comparison; creates action items
Requires calendar discipline; can feel bureaucratic for small teams
Alert-only monitoring
Automatic; no meeting overhead
Misses gradual degradation; no user satisfaction check; no tech debt review
Informal team retrospective
Low overhead; good team dynamics
No quantitative metric review; action items often lost
Sprint retrospective only
Already scheduled; familiar format
Too infrequent for T+1 day check; process-focused not metric-focused
Security and Reliability

Review documents may contain sensitive performance data, cost figures, and incident descriptions. Store them in access-controlled spaces (Confluence with team-level permissions, Google Drive with org-restricted sharing).
The go/no-go decision must be explicit and documented with a timestamp. If the feature is later found to have caused harm, the review record is the evidence of due diligence.
Capture the baseline metrics before any launch — pre-launch capture is a reliability requirement, not optional. Post-launch review without a baseline produces opinions, not measurements.
Mental Model
Think of a feature launch as a
controlled experiment with three measurement checkpoints

. T+1 day checks whether the experiment is safe to continue. T+1 week checks whether it is working as intended. T+30 days checks whether it produced the business outcome it was designed for. Each checkpoint has a binary
go/hold
decision. Without these checkpoints, a feature launch is not an experiment — it is a release with no learning mechanism attached.
Key Takeaways
Three reviews at T+1 day, T+1 week, and T+30 day serve different purposes: stability check, metrics validation, and business impact measurement.
Capture baseline metrics before launch — post-launch review without a baseline produces opinions, not measurements.
Always report latency at P50, P95, and P99 — for AI systems, P99 can be 5–10× P50 and represents a significant fraction of real user experience.
Define go/no-go criteria before launch so the expansion decision is data-driven, not negotiated in the review meeting.
Inventory technical debt taken during launch at the T+1 week review — explicit tracking prevents "temporary" shortcuts from becoming permanent.
Every review must produce an action item list with owners and due dates — a review that produces only observations has no value.
The T+30 day review's feature expansion go/no-go is the governance gate that prevents a shaky launch from being quietly expanded without a deliberate decision.
